title: Violence Against Latina Immigrants: Citizenship, Inequality, and Community By Roberta Villalon New York University Press. 2010. 206 pages. $23 paper
abstract: In this insightful volume, Villalón documents in depth how race, gender, sex­uality, class ideologies, among other regimes of inequality, seep through the formulation and implementation of immigration law, even in the case of laws that have been created with the purpose to protect victims of intimate partner violence. In Villalón's convincing argument, articulated and solidly deployed throughout the book, such ideologies and hierarchies do not simply shape how laws are written and executed, but how the laws that are developed and carried out in turn contribute to reproduce such hierarchies, an outcome that undermines the objectives of these laws and contradicts their inclusive intentions. Villalón's clear argument is based on 2 years of fieldwork, in-depth interviews with immigrants and case workers as well as archival materials and secondary sources. This fieldwork included working part-time as a volunteer intern at an organization that provided legal services to low-income immigrants who qualified to apply for legalization through the Violence Against Women Act (VAWA) and the Victims of Trafficking and Violence Protection Act (VTVPA). Through her position at this organization, Villalón was able to interact with the applicants, as she helped screen them and prepared their files, and to observe the attorneys and legal case workers. This position gave her invaluable access to the views, perspectives, and interpretations that the applicants and the legal workers had of the legalization process and how they maneuver through it.
